checks.a_analysis: I have compared every section heading and paragraph in the output JSON with the source PDF. The output accurately reflects the text of the form, including the headers, instructions, and numbered sections. No fabricated content or placeholder text was identified.

I verified the address '952 Old US Highway 70, Black Mountain, NC 28711', the phone number '1-888-349-7715', and all four URLs (ncoshr.gov, hepinc.com, oah.state.nc.us, ncdhhs.gov) against the source document. All contact information is identical to the source.

The source document consists of 9 pages and 22 numbered sections (with section 22 appearing twice due to a numbering error in the original). The output JSON contains all sections and reflects the full content of the 9-page inquiry.

The source document contains no formal data tables. It is a structured form with fields, which the JSON conversion correctly represents as textual elements or list items. As no tables are present, there are no row omissions.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I examined the content of complex sections, such as section 16 (Needs Assessment), 19c (Building Information counts), and section 22 (Supplemental Information). The output captures all descriptive text, instructions, and specific data points requested in the original form.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording in the output JSON matches the source PDF exactly. No substantive wording changes, number alterations, or date errors were found. The conversion is a high-fidelity representation of the source text.

checks.a_analysis: The output duplicates the text of section titles by including them both in the section's 'title' property and as a redundant 'heading' element within the section's content. This occurs for sections 1, 6, and 13. For example, in Section 1, the title property is '1. AGENCY NAME:' and the first element is a heading with text '1. AGENCY NAME: ____________________________________________________________________________'. Similar duplication occurs in sections 6 and 13.
checks.b_evidence_source:
1. AGENCY NAME: ____________________________________________________________________________
6. SIGNATURE OF LICENSEE OR PERSON WITH SIGNATORY AUTHORITY: The undersigned...
13. EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR’S EDUCATIONAL EXPERIENCE*: Attach additional pages if necessary.
checks.b_evidence_output:
"title": "1. AGENCY NAME:", "text": "1. AGENCY NAME: ____________________________________________________________________________"
"title": "6. SIGNATURE OF LICENSEE OR PERSON WITH SIGNATORY AUTHORITY:", "text": "6. SIGNATURE OF LICENSEE OR PERSON WITH SIGNATORY AUTHORITY: The undersigned..."
"title": "13. EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR’S EDUCATIONAL EXPERIENCE*: Attach additional pages if necessary.", "text": "13. EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR’S EDUCATIONAL EXPERIENCE*:"

checks.a_analysis: The heading hierarchy is inconsistent and broken. While the document starts with an H2 title, only sections 1, 6, and 13 are assigned H3 heading elements. All other top-level sections (2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22) lack heading elements entirely, appearing only as metadata in the section title property. This prevents screen reader users from navigating the full document structure via headings, as most major sections are omitted from the heading map.
checks.b_evidence_source: Sections 1 through 22 are all formatted as major section headers in the source document.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 1 contains 'heading-2' (Level 3). Section 2 contains only a 'paragraph' element. Section 6 contains 'heading-3' (Level 3). Section 7 contains only a 'paragraph' element.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is a form and does not contain formal tables. The layout of form fields (e.g., City, Zip Code, County) is correctly preserved as paragraphs in the output.
The reading order follows the source document's logical sequence from Section 1 to Section 22 accurately.
The bullet point in Section 1 is correctly converted into a 'list' type. Other form-based options and checkboxes are appropriately rendered as paragraph text.
All URLs found in the source document (sections 13 and 22) are correctly identified and present as clickable links in the output with the correct 'href' attributes.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML rendering follows the single-column layout of the original 9-page PDF form closely. Sections are numbered and ordered correctly, matching the source document. The transition from a multi-page PDF to a continuous HTML page is handled well without losing the visual organization of the form fields and instructions. The layout accurately reflects the intent of the original form.

The document does not contain traditional complex data tables with grid lines. However, it uses structured repetitive blocks for data entry (e.g., Section 11 for owners, Section 18 for references, Section 20 for inspections). These blocks are rendered consistently in the HTML, preserving the logical grouping of fields (Name, Address, City, State, Zip, etc.) found in the source PDF, ensuring a user can easily understand and fill out the form.

The source PDF contains no meaningful images such as logos, photos, or charts. It primarily consists of text and form elements like lines and checkboxes. The checkboxes are correctly represented in the HTML as visual indicators. No broken image icons or missing meaningful visual elements were observed in the rendered output.

There is no evidence of body content being duplicated in the HTML output. The repetition of field blocks in sections like 11, 13, 18, and 20 is intentional and matches the source PDF exactly. The inclusion of section titles in the 'Contents' navigation area at the top of the HTML is an expected accessibility feature and is not considered content duplication.
